data.title: Netflix: Hold The Stock After The Earnings Results
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) is one of the best-known entertainment service providers globally, operating in as many as 190 countries. The company started out by offering TV series, documentaries, and feature films, but quickly grew into the games scene as well.

Over the past 5 years, NFLX's stock price saw quite a bit of volatility. In the first half of 2022, Netflix lost roughly 80% of its market value. The turnaround in the sentiment came quickly, however, and the stock price kept gradually increasing until mid-2025, significantly outperforming the broader market (SPY). In the past quarters, sentiment once again turned negative, and the share price cratered.

As NFLX recently reported its quarterly earnings results, the aim of this article today is to give a balanced view, whether it is worth investing in NFLX's stock now or not. My arguments are going to be revolving around the macroeconomic environment, and around company-specific metrics, including growth, profitability, and valuation.

Financials

Revenue

Netflix beat analyst estimates on both the top and bottom lines in the most recent quarter. Revenues came in at $12.05B, $80 million above analyst estimates, representing a growth of 17.6% year-over-year. The drivers of the revenue growth were membership growth, higher pricing, and increased ad revenue. The firm achieved higher revenue growth than anticipated, primarily due to the better-than-expected membership growth and ad revenue growth, partially offset by an unfavorable FX environment. To put membership growth into perspective, let us look at some numbers. Netflix now 325M million subscriptions - equivalent to about 1 billion viewers. These viewers are also watching more content, as the number of hours watched is up by 2% year-over-year in the second half of 2025. Lastly, Netflix's original content is gaining more and more traction. Viewing of Netflix originals was up 9% YoY. Non-branded hours, however, slightly declined as a result of the WGA strike in 2023-2024.

In my view, these figures are excellent, in light of the current macroeconomic environment. Netflix is essentially selling entertainment products, which are considered to be discretionary and nonessential. During times of low consumer confidence, when people are less certain about their financial outlook, they normally cut back on discretionary spending. Despite consumer sentiment still being just above the 10-year lows, people do not seem to be cutting on their Netflix subscriptions. Actually, exactly the opposite is happening; membership is growing faster than anticipated. Maybe Netflix is the budget entertainment option, as people do not want to spend larger sums by going out?

Let us look at the revenue growth breakdown regionally. I find it appealing that all of the regions are exhibiting double-digit growth. Growth accelerated significantly in the U.S. and Canada, reaching 18% YoY, while growth in the EMEA remained stable at 18%, but showed a slight decline on an FX-neutral basis. These two regions remain the largest by revenue. LATAM growth also accelerated, reaching 15%, while the APAC growth significantly slowed, but still remained in the higher teens.

Looking forward, Netflix expects revenue growth to be between 12% and 14% in 2026. This is somewhat lower than what they achieved in the previous years, and probably the primary reason why the stock price fell right after the earnings release. Q1 guidance also came in below expectations.

Does it justify the sell-off, though? In my view, no. The firm is having a large number of initiatives to keep up the growth. They are planning to expand their live events offering with events like the World Baseball Classic in Japan; they plan to expand into video podcasts, and also expand their gaming business. At the same time, ad revenue in 2026 is expected to double. Creating more high quality original content to strengthen the core business and the acquisition of Warner Bros. should also boost the firm's financial performance in the coming year.

Profitability

It is not only the revenue growth that impresses, but also Netflix's profit margins. If we look at the past three years, we can see that NFLX managed to improve its profitability metrics significantly.

Over the past year alone, operating margin expanded by 3 points. Looking forward, in Q1 the company expects the operating margin to be 32.1%, while for the whole year they anticipate 31.5%.

All in all, I find Netflix's business attractive from a growth and business fundamental perspective.

Valuation

To assess whether NFLX is a good buy at the current price levels, let us look at a set of traditional price multiples. The following table compares NFLX's metrics with those of the communication services sector median, as well as with its own historic averages.

We can clearly see that NFLX's stock is trading at a significant premium compared to the sector as a whole, but at a substantial discount to its historic averages.

Let us look a bit deeper into this. If we compare NFLX's current metrics to its past metrics, I believe that the discount is justified. While growth remains strong, the growth is slowing. Normally, slowing growth leads to multiple compressions; therefore, it is understandable why the stock is cheaper now than it was in past years.

The comparison to the sector as a whole, however, may be somewhat misleading, as it contains a large number of firms, which may have entirely different business models from NFLX's. So let us narrow down the peer group to the movies and entertainment industry only. The following table contains valuation, profitability, and growth metrics. Undoubtedly the firm's profitability and growth metrics are appealing, with one of the strongest net profit margins and one of the strongest EPS FWD growth metrics in the industry. The valuation, however, appears stretched, especially looking at the P/CF and P/S metrics.

While I believe a certain premium is justified, I think that the stock is still somewhat expensive, especially considering the negative sentiment around the firm and the recent downward momentum. I would like to see the stock price fall another 10% to 20%, before I would consider it a buy from a valuation point of view.

That being said, I would also like to point out that the firm is returning significant value to its shareholders through share buybacks. During the quarter, they bought back 18.9M shares for $2.1B, and another $8 billion remains from their buyback program. While it is a small amount compared to their market cap, it might be an indication that management believes that the shares are attractively valued.

Risks

Let me highlight a few risks, which I believe are key when considering an investment in Netflix's business.

1. Competition. Netflix themselves said that they are not necessarily competing against other firms in the industry, but competing against all activities that people do. I completely agree with this statement.

Consumer preferences evolve quickly. I believe one has to provide quality content continuously to remain competitive and keep the attention of people. As discussed earlier in the article, I am also not quite sure, if people would be so engaged with Netflix, if they were financially healthier. Would they prefer going out, attending concerts, or going on excursions, instead of watching content on Netflix?

2. Content Netflix does create a large number of original content, but their success is mainly driven by a handful of shows that became fan favorites, including Stranger Things. Not all of their movies and series are able to gain so much traction and attention. How often are they able to create such viral content, and how long can they keep running certain fan-favorite shows before they become boring? Again, consumer preferences evolve quickly, and if they are not able to keep up, their growth might slow significantly.

Conclusions

Netflix beat analyst estimates on the top and bottom lines, despite slowing growth figures. Revenue kept growing across all regions, driven by membership growth and ad revenue growth, while profitability improved. Double-digit growth is still expected for the 2026 full year.

From a valuation point of view, I am more cautious about the firm. I would like to see the share price fall 10% to 20% before I would consider the stock a value play.

Risks include competition and changing preferences of the consumer.

Overall, I rate NFLX as a hold.

data.title: Microbot Medical: High-Risk High-Return Stock, Still Hard To Value
data.description:
Microbot Medical (MBOT) is a robotic medical tech (MedTech) company I have been observing for a while. It developed the world’s first single-use remotely-operated robotic tech, LIBERTY, that allows the streamlining of endovascular interventions that enable minimally invasive procedures and reduced radiation exposure to clinicians. Its business model is B2B, where it primarily sells its solution to hospitals and clinics aiming to reduce high CAPEX associated with traditional surgical robots.

Share performance has been volatile since it went public in 1992, with the stock trading below its historical highs for the most part. Nonetheless, despite being down -1.42% YTD, the stock has seen some tailwinds as of late, with 14.3% upside when we zoom out and view it from a 1-year return standpoint. It currently trades around $2 per share.

I initiate my coverage with a neutral rating. My 1-year price target of $1.9 suggests that MBOT is fully valued.

Financial Reviews

As in the case of any company developing novel technologies, MBOT has largely been a pre-revenue company with zero revenues. Nonetheless, the growth outlook appears compelling.

In Q3 2025, MBOT seems to have entered the first step into revenue generation given the FDA 510(k) clearance of LIBERTY. With the Limited Market Release [LMR] taking place as of late, MBOT has effectively commenced its first commercial activity.

As of Q3 2025, MBOT was unprofitable, much due to the same reason I mentioned. In Q3 2025, net loss was $3.6 million, slightly similar to where it was the same time last year at $3.22 million. In my opinion, while net loss widened, MBOT appears to have done sufficiently well to maintain loss levels YoY, especially when the company today is at the stage where it is hard to judge based on profitability alone. Meanwhile, the increased spending for Q3 was also justified due to the company entering the first step of commercialization. In Q3, SG&A expenses represented the biggest drawback to the bottom line, possibly as MBOT ramped up commercial teams and 3PL logistics to support its upcoming nationwide sales efforts.

Overall, liquidity is decent. MBOT ended Q3 2025 with around $80 million of current assets, which includes cash and marketable securities.

Such a significant increase from 2024's $5 million range was much due to the common stock issuance done in 2025. MBOT seems to have reinvested the proceeds into marketable securities. With operating cash flow [OCF] being in the red historically, MBOT has been getting its liquidity from financing cash flows.

Catalyst

In my opinion, catalysts remain moderate to high, especially with MBOT sitting at the narrative transition between R&D and a commercial-driven growth story. As such, the main catalyst here is the transition from LMR to Full Market Release [FMR] in Q2 2026 at the Society of Interventional Radiology [SIR] conference:

The company is expected to initiate the limited market release of the LIBERTY System during the current fourth quarter of 2025 with the broader launch anticipated to occur in April 2026 during the Society of Interventional Radiology conference.

Source: Seeking Alpha

The launch during the conference also marks the company’s entrance into a broader sales effort nationwide to drive more than $4 million of projected revenue in FY 2026 as per analysts’ estimates.

The relatively growing market in the treatment of peripheral artery disease [PAD] in the US, which requires endovascular procedures, as per the research from Polaris, suggests that there is an attractive blue-ocean opportunity for a disposable single-use robotic tech.

Risk

Overall, I believe that MBOT is a high-risk, high-return, pre-revenue opportunity for investors. With most of the focus on Q2 2026 FMR, the biggest risk would be that of execution. Even if MBOT could demonstrate success with its pilot project with Emory Hospital, broader sales efforts of a novel technology such as MBOT’s robotic platform may involve potential drawbacks from the lack of market readiness to adopt the technology itself. Meanwhile, I also consider managing a larger volume of sales to be MBOT’s next test, especially with the system being single-use, requiring careful logistics planning to fulfill reordering.

Another thing I notice that could present a bottleneck for the stock to trend higher is the relatively high number of warrants due to the company raising sizable capital from common stock issuance in 2025. With the exercise price of the 2025 issued warrants ranging roughly between $1.5 and $2, I may expect MBOT to face selling pressure from the existing holders if share price increases, preferably from MBOT seeing a rating upgrade into 2026 upon the FMR at the SIR conference.

Valuation / Pricing

My target price for MBOT is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, based on the consensus analysis:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions - MBOT’s LIBERTY sees rapid adoption across major hospital networks in the US upon the successful FMR in Q2 2026, disrupting the endovascular procedure industry with its single-use disposable platform. I estimate the number of total shares, including dilution, to be 68 million.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - MBOT sees sluggish sales activities, primarily driven by supply chain issues related to reordering processes across hospitals. MBOT sees wider net losses as the expenses to build up a nationwide commercial sales team outweigh the revenue generation. I assume higher shares dilution to 90 million as MBOT raises more capital from common stock issuance.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $1.9 per share. At this point, I would conclude that it remains challenging to properly value MBOT as a pre-revenue company, as my projection yields a figure that suggests MBOT is fully-valued today. I give the stock a neutral rating.

Conclusion

MBOT is potentially transitioning into a commercial growth story in 2026. Given the FMR, there is enough catalyst for the stock for a re-rating. Execution remains important across sales as well as supply chain, considering the single-use robotic novel technology. The risk remains high, since expansion from a pilot into a broader nationwide sales entails a whole set of new challenges. While MBOT has enough liquidity to support all these, the capital raising last year has also resulted in a lot of outstanding warrants that could create selling pressures on MBOT. My analysis suggests that it remains tough to value MBOT. I rate the stock neutral.

data.title: Collective Mining: Momentum Gains For Geologically Sought-After Metals In Colombia's Caldas Region
data.description:
The Market Rightly Praises Collective Mining Ltd.

This article is about Collective Mining Ltd. (CNL) listed on the NYSE.

It is not unusual for the market to react impulsively to US stocks, but Collective Mining Ltd. shares are currently displaying a rational stance in our opinion, with the positive sentiment among participants driving the share price up 212.5% ​​over the past 12 months at the time of writing.

The Metal Price Context Is Of The Upward Trend Type

The company plans to develop extensive precious metals deposits, including copper, in a region of Colombia with a centuries-old mining tradition. Gold and silver are trading at record highs due to their safe haven status and are in high demand among investors during the current geopolitical crisis. Being a safe haven due to fears of US devaluation and uncertainty is now being driven by fears of financial risk too, as assessed by US analysts.

Gold and Silver Outlook

As of this writing, the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) is $4,755.39, up 10% year-to-date and 75.3% over the past twelve months.

Analysts at UBS Global Wealth Management forecast a gold price of $5,000 per ounce in the coming months, possibly even $5,400 per ounce.

Silver Spot Price (XAGUSD:CUR) is $93.79, up 31.6% year-to-date and 206.8% over the past twelve months. Geopolitical tensions and concerns that supply cannot meet strong industrial and investment demand have contributed to this increase. Seeking Alpha writes:

Against this backdrop, analysts at Citigroup raised their three-month forecasts for gold and silver to $5,000 an ounce and $100 an ounce, respectively.

Copper Outlook

Copper, on the other hand, has always been highly valued for its properties as a preferred and most sought-after metal, ideal for electrification projects. With regard to electrification goals, the world's economies are in fierce competition. Recent events have shown that even long-standing alliances, such as those across the Atlantic, are being tested. So much for prioritizing shared strategic geopolitical interests, values, and principles: partners who until recently got along well are now realizing that their relationships are far from optimal when it comes to representing their respective positions in regions with large deposits of various metals and minerals, such as Greenland.

Copper Futures (HG1:COM) are at $5.78 (or $12,743/tonne), up 2.4% year-to-date and 34.6% over the past twelve months.

For Goldman Sachs, the copper price rally has already paid off, Seeking Alpha reported last Thursday, but it also wrote in the same report:

Many analysts remain bullish on copper, pointing to long-anticipated supply shortfalls as the use of the metal in the new energy and artificial intelligence sectors revives demand lost to China's property crash.

Tailwinds for Collective Mining: The Stock in Focus

Collective Mining shares are likely to benefit from the current upward market trend, driven by growing interest in commodities, particularly those the company explores itself. The company's management stands out as one that will be highlighted in this analysis, demonstrating a clear understanding of what needs to be done. The positive performance of precious metals and copper could reinforce this process and generate further momentum. Risks are still part of the equation, and one should not forget that, but they are easier to manage in an uptrend, as positive momentum, when sustained, mitigates short-term fluctuations.

About Collective Mining

Collective Mining, headquartered in Toronto, Canada, is a gold, silver, copper, and tungsten exploration company in Caldas, Colombia, a region renowned for its exploration and mining activities, as we can see from the presentation.

Relevant gold and silver mines are already located in the area. We assume that Collective Mining is primarily interested in these two precious metals.

Productions in the Surroundings: Buriticá

Buriticá, located 60 km northwest of Medellín, Colombia, is an underground gold, silver, and copper mine. It is wholly owned by Continental Gold Limited Sucursal Colombia, a company founded by the same team that created Collective Mining and later sold Continental to Zijin Mining for approximately US$2 billion.

Mining Intelligence and News (“MDO”) says:

Buriticá Gold Mine is one of Colombia’s largest and highest-grade gold mines, and the country’s first large-scale modern underground operation. The project includes the Buriticá Mine and Processing Plant, supporting a long mine life. Continental Gold Colombia plans to expand capacity to 5,000 tpd by 2028.

The website of Zijin Mining Group Co., Limited lists gold deposits of 333 tonnes at a grade of 8.21 g/t and silver deposits of 1,000 tonnes. These mineral resource figures (including mineral reserves) correspond to a gold content of 10,700,023 troy ounces and a silver content of 32,150,747 troy ounces. Also, the Chinese miner’s website indicates that the Buriticá gold mine produced 10 tonnes of gold and 26 tonnes of silver in 2024, which equates to an annual production of 321,508 troy ounces of gold and 836,801 troy ounces of silver.

Productions in the Surroundings: Segovia

The Segovia project, an active underground mine for gold, silver, zinc, and lead, located 132 km northeast of Medellín, Colombia, is wholly owned by Aris Mining Corporation.

MDO indirectly adds to the mineral richness of the Collective Mining's same district and notes:

Approximately half of the operation's production is sourced from mill feed purchased from CMPs sourced from within and outside of the Aris' mining titles.

From the Aris Mining website, we derived a definition of Contract Mining Partners (CMPs) and a quantification of this type of economic cooperation in the mining sector in Segovia, Caldas. These are local, small-scale mining companies. In Segovia alone, the CMPs employ over 3,500 miners, with each company typically having between 50 and 500 workers. They have long-term contracts with Aris Mining for the supply of processing material. This is economically and commercially advantageous for both parties under Colombian mining and environmental law.

Plenty of feed in Caldas; the mill's feed supply is to expand.

MDO adds:

On June 30, 2025, Aris Mining announces the successful commissioning of a second processing mill at its high-grade Segovia Operations. This expansion increases Segovia's processing capacity by 50%, from 2,000 to 3,000 tonnes per day (tpd).

Additional resources support the expansion in the region. In its CNW release dated January 8, 2026, Aris Mining updated its mineral reserve and resource estimates for Segovia as follows:

From 2024 estimates:

  • Measured and Indicated mineral resources (“M&I”): Up 7% to 3.6 million ounces of gold (Moz).
  • Proven and Probable mineral reserves (“P&P”): Up 12% to 1.5 Moz.
  • Inferred resources: Up 12% to 2.9 Moz.
  • Exceptional High Metal Content: 15.3 g/t for M&I and 10.7 g/t for P&P.

Neil Woodyer, CEO of Aris Mining, told CNW:

Segovia continues to provide a strong foundation of high-margin production and long-term value creation for Aris Mining. […] The Company selected a gold price of $3,200 per ounce for mineral resources and $2,800 per ounce for mineral reserves. […] The sensitivity analysis demonstrates […] the high-grade nature and strong underlying economics of the deposit.

Aris Mining's website indicates that gold has been mined in the historic Segovia mining district for over 150 years. Also, in 2024, Segovia produced 187,583 ounces of gold. With the commissioning of a second processing plant in June 2025, Segovia will gradually increase its annual gold production from 210,000–250,000 ounces in 2025 to approximately 300,000 ounces by 2026. Thanks to the expanded processing plant, Aris's progress in underground development and the mill feed purchases from its own CMPs are thus sustainable.

Productions in the Surroundings: El Roble mine

The El Roble mine is an active underground copper, gold, and silver mine located 72 km southwest of Medellín, Colombia. It is wholly owned and operated by Minera El Roble S.A., 90% of which is owned by Atico Mining Corporation (ATCMF) (ATY:CA), which is probably better known to investors than Minera El Roble due to its listing on the North American stock exchange. About the El Roble Mine, MDO states:

It is assumed that the mine will close by 2027, although management anticipates potential extensions.

Management's expectations regarding a production extension were strongly confirmed by GLOBE NEWSWIRE on November 24, 2025, when Atico Mining Corporation announced positive results for 19 drilling operations completed in 2025 to expand El Roble production.

Fernando E. Ganoza, CEO of Atico Mining Corporation, said:

These assay results continue to increase confidence in our view that additional high-grade copper and gold mineralization remains both within the historically defined bodies and beyond the previously outlined mineralized shell and are open at depth and along strike. The drill campaign is planned to continue throughout 2025.

Since Atico Mining Corporation acquired the El Roble mine in 2013,

“Atico has more than doubled its mineral processing capacity, from 400 to 1,000 metric tons/day.”, writes Euan Wallace for Mongabay on December 8, 2025, adding some of the mine's production figures to date.

In fact, the journalist also reports:

[…] El Roble remains a prominent project in Colombia’s push for transition minerals. In 2024, the mine produced 13.7 million pounds of copper, an increase of 3% over 2023. El Roble aims to expand its operation across the wider municipality of El Carmen de Atrato, listing 21 prospective exploration targets across its mineral concessions covering 6,355 hectares (15,700 acres).

Caldas Projects of Collective Mining: Guayabales and San Antonio

Within this mining district, Collective Mining (hereinafter, we may also refer to it as “CNL”) operates two main projects:

Guayabales Project

Collective Mining describes this flagship project in its portfolio as follows. In the January 19, 2026, announcement naming Mr. Santos as Executive Vice President, the Company states:

Guayabales, is anchored by the Apollo system, which hosts the large-scale, bulk-tonnage and high-grade gold-silver-copper-tungsten Apollo system.

But there's more to it than that:

The near-surface gold-silver-copper-tungsten (Au-Ag-Cu-W) deposit exhibits shallow mineralization. Below this, in deeper strata approximately 1,000 meters below the surface, lies a specific geological system known in the Apollo project target area as the “Reduced Intrusion Related System.” This geological environment is known to geologists for the presence of certain minerals that—similar to the Apollo system—make the Guayabales project very rich in gold (Au), silver (Ag), bismuth (Bi), and tellurium (Te). Collective Mining has identified this system within the Apollo target area as the "Ramp Zone" (red area in the screenshot below). Drilling results in this ramp zone are characterized by the interception of high-grade gold mineralization with notable intercepts. Examples include 24.80 meters grading 29.42 g/t gold within 63.90 meters grading 13.46 g/t gold at a depth of approximately 400 meters from the start of the Ramp Zone and 18.85 meters grading 19.39 g/t gold within 57.65 meters grading 7.83 g/t gold at a depth of approximately 100 meters from the start of the Ramp Zone.

Exploration Targets Beyond the Apollo Area and Known Zones

In the January 19, 2026, announcement appointing Mr. Santos as EVP, the company states:

The Company's objectives at the Guayabales Project are to expand the newly discovered high-grade Ramp Zone along strike and to depth and drill a series of greenfield generated targets on the property.

In fact, within its Guayabales project, Collective Mining has identified several exploration targets beyond the Apollo target area and known zones, where the company intends to expand its exploration efforts to discover additional mineralization.

These targets are:

The Trap Target: a target area discovered in 2024 where drilling intersected high-grade mineralization with a gold equivalent of 1.10 g/t over a length of 632.25 meters. This is the company's second significant discovery in Guayabales in 2024. It is a potentially rich and extensive zone of mineralization. Modeling suggests a possible copper-gold porphyry system to the north, but along a strike of 1.4 kilometers, the mineralization appears to extend further south as well.

The Plutus target area is located approximately 600 meters east of the Apollo system in the southern part of a 1,000 x 750 meter target area. Collective Mining is focused on copper-gold porphyry, which to date has been characterized by pronounced soil and rock anomalies, including copper concentrations exceeding 300 ppm, gold grades exceeding 0.20 g/t, and molybdenum values ​​exceeding 20 ppm (see screenshot below). Exploration drilling at Plutus indicates the presence of a porphyry system, which is being further investigated through drilling. Drilling is also targeting the northern Plutus discovery, as promising results from this deposit, discovered in 2023, underscore the mineralization potential in this area as well.

New Greenfield Exploration Targets at the Guayabales Project: 'The X Target’

Collective Mining Ltd. has identified several new greenfield exploration targets within its Guayabales project, including the “The X target” area.

The “X target” area appears to exhibit significant silver mineralization. Of particular note are the 12.85-meter intervals grading 503 g/t silver equivalent, which underscores the high potential of this target area. This potential is enormous, as the "X target" area is largely undeveloped and represents a remarkable addition to Collective Mining's portfolio. It enables the exploration of other commodities beyond gold and copper, especially opening access to the silver market.

The San Antonio Project and Its Three Main Targets

In the January 19, 2026 announcement appointing Mr. Santos as EVP, Collective Mining describes this project as follows:

the Company has launched its largest drilling campaign in history at its optioned San Antonio Project (100% potential interest) as it hunts for new discoveries and looks to expand upon the newly discovered porphyry system at the Pound target. The San Antonio Project is located between two to five kilometers east-northeast of the Guayabales Project and could potentially share infrastructure given their proximity to each other.

Collective Mining has identified the following primary targets in San Antonio:

Pound Target Area: Drilling in this target area has intersected significant mineralization, including a 290.20-meter interval grading 0.84 g/t gold equivalent. Based on these initial indications of mineralization, Collective Mining is likely to access at least four commodity markets: gold, copper, silver, and molybdenum.

Additional targets are located within a 2.5 km radius: the Euro Target Area and the COP Target Area. This indicates that there may be scope for exploration activities over a much larger area where amazing gold and other precious metal mineralization may occur.

Collective Mining Ltd. on the NYSE

Collective Mining Ltd.’s NYSE-listed shares under the symbol “CNL” were trading at $15.67/share, giving it a market capitalization of $1.39 billion, as of this writing.

Technical Analysis

The current stock price is 49% above its 52-week average of $10.53 (range: $4.26 to $16.80). The 20-day average is above the 50-day average, which in turn is above the 200-day average. This suggests that CNL stock is not only in a short-term uptrend but also exhibits factors that point to a sustained uptrend. In mid-December 2025, there was a brief attempt by the bears to gain the upper hand when the 20-day average fell below the 50-day average. However, this attempt was short-lived due to volatility and may have presented an opportunity to increase one's position in the stock. The RSI reading of 62.84 indicates that the stock is trading in the upper half of its range. Although the distance to overbought territory is smaller than in December, significant upside potential remains. A subsequent downtrend could lead to a price decline and thus a more favorable entry point. For existing CNL shareholders, waiting for a price drop is a more sensible option than for potential new buyers. There is positive momentum in the metals that Collective Mining continues to explore, and drilling is delivering impressive and promising results. These indicate large mineral deposits in a basin, suggesting rich deposits deep underground, along strike, and particularly across the Caldas mining district. The discovery of this mineralization is noteworthy, and based on these factors, we consider a sharp price decline, even from an overbought level, unlikely.

Valuation

Collective Mining Ltd. raises capital to finance the ongoing development of the Guayabales Project. The funds will be used for exploration, technical studies, and, in particular, underground development in the Ramp Zone, where the company intends to develop the known gold deposits. The capital increase was achieved through an upsized “bought deal” public offering of common shares. On October 8, 2025, the block deal of 6,600,000 common shares at a price of C$19.00 per share (approximately US$13.62 per share) was successfully completed for gross proceeds of C$125,400,000. The offering price was near the daily high of US$13.60 on the NYSE. Suppose CNL now issues new common shares. This action would coincide with improved prospects for CNL's mineral projects, particularly the Guayabales Ramp Zone, which currently can boast proceeds of C$125.4 million. CNL shares listed on the NYSE would command a higher value on the stock market than before, when all material factors were less favorable. The market reacted enthusiastically to CNL shares in October 2025, making it possible to raise capital; logically, that opportunity should also exist now. The stock is considered more valuable because the funds raised are reinvested in exploration, potentially leading to new discoveries that could unlock further gold and silver reserves. Consider this: C$125.4 million has the potential to reveal more than the C$73.7 million in cash CNL held as of September 30, 2025. Moreover, given the ongoing geopolitical competition, demand for copper is far from exhausted, making CNL's copper assets even more strategic. Given the current situation, wouldn't these factors be a good reason to buy Collective Mining stock? Additionally, a weaker U.S. dollar relative to major currencies worldwide means that international investors would have greater purchasing power when buying CNL shares, fueling demand for NYSE-listed CNL shares. Also, Collective Mining's Guayabales and San Antonio projects are located relatively close to many profitable and rich metal deposits, including Aris Mining's Segovia mine in Colombia's (kind of a) "Gold Triangle" in the Caldas Department. The concentration of valuable mineral resources in the Segovia region is considered an important benchmark. Should Segovia, even at a significantly lower gold price of US$2,800 per ounce for reserves, guarantee high-margin production and long-term value creation for Aris Mining, a similar sensitivity analysis regarding ore quality and the economic foundations of the deposits targeted by Collective Mining would also be a criterion that would prompt large investors to purchase Collective Mining shares – and presumably not for the short run.

The Risk Section

Risk of illegal or unauthorized mining activities: This appears to be a local problem, as evidenced by complaints from Collective Mining's neighbors, and could pose a threat to the proper conduct of the exploration activities of Collective Mining's exploration team. As of this writing, MDO states:

Illegal mining around the site has continued since 2022 due to limited local enforcement, posing risks of reserve losses, production interruptions, and financial impact.

Risk of Discrimination in the Treatment of Foreign Investors: For example, as of this writing, MDO states:

In 2024, Continental Gold Inc. launched international arbitration against Colombia under the Canada–Colombia FTA and ICSID Convention, claiming unfair treatment of its investment. The case remains in the written stage, with hearings expected in late 2026.

But Zijin Mining Group, the buyer and main shareholder of Continental Gold Inc., is a Chinese company. This arbitration with the Colombian authorities can also be interpreted in different ways, given the competition in the global commodities market between Western economies and China, and Collective Mining is a Canadian company lining up with Westerners. Conclusions to be drawn, for you.

Risk of Low Liquid Stock: Collective Mining is an illiquid stock on the NYSE; its average trading volume (3 months) was 58,670, as reported by Seeking Alpha under “Trading Data” at the time of writing. Excessive holdings carry the risk that, in the event of a sale, investors may have to drastically reduce their selling price due to unforeseen circumstances, largely below the average purchase price. This is due to the low daily trading volume, which makes it difficult to execute investor orders. Conversely, the very low liquidity can also mean that the price of Collective Mining shares can rise sharply during periods of strong investor demand due to limited availability.

Liquidity (Cash) Risk: Operating cash flow in the September quarter of 2025 was C$13.4 million, plus capital expenditures of C$10.9 million. Assuming that this operating cash flow and mineral project expenditures remain constant over a twelve-month period, Collective Mining will require C$97.2 million in the twelve months beginning September 30, 2025. As of September 30, 2025, the company had cash and cash equivalents of C$71.3 million and gross proceeds of C$125.4 million from the October 2025 bought deal previously mentioned. Collective Mining will have financial autonomy for approximately two years beginning in the September quarter of 2025. Rising metal prices and progress on key mineral projects are creating a positive market sentiment for the company, enabling it to raise additional capital through the issuance of common shares on the stock market. Given this development, lenders will be encouraged to join investors and provide loans to Collective Mining. We currently assess the liquidity risk as low.

Conclusion

Collective Mining Ltd., whose shares are traded on the NYSE, is enjoying a positive market sentiment, which appears justified given the precious metals and copper exploration company's progress in the Caldas district of Colombia. The company is experiencing growing market interest as it is able to raise substantial capital, led by major investors who believe in the promising mineral potential of its projects in Guayabales and San Antonio. Given these developments, the positive outlook for CNL shares is hard to refute.

data.title: VanEck Mid-January 2026 Bitcoin ChainCheck
data.description:
Bitcoin rallied with renewed ETP inflows and a decoupling from equities, while miners pivot to AI and undervalued DATs ripen for consolidation.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) was a strong performer over the last 30 days, returning (+12%) as trailing 30-day volatility fell (-29%) to (Vol = 27). Bitcoin’s lack of energy over the past 30 days sapped volatility, bringing it to levels just below the 13th percentile over the past year. After what many would describe as “tax loss harvesting,” driving early December sales, which actually seemed to start in October this year, Bitcoin prices melted up through the first half of January.

We attribute Bitcoin’s buoyancy to a host of factors, including softer inflation readings, Fed independence fears, CLARITY Act optimism, and generally oversold conditions. This optimism catalyzed ETP inflows of $440M over the past 30 days, compared with outflows of -$1.3B over the prior 30-day period. In fact, between 1/12/2026 and 1/14/2026, BTC ETP inflows were +$1.66B.

As measured by 30-day correlation, Bitcoin became untethered from the S&P 500, reaching 0.18, which corresponds to the 9th percentile over the past year. This is the lowest correlation reading for BTC/SP500 since October 2025. Meanwhile, BTC moved more in lockstep with gold, with the 30-day correlation reaching 0.28, which ranks a tad above the 80th percentile over the last year.

The positive price action in Bitcoin over the past 30 days translated into (+7%) growth in open interest in BTC to reach $32.4B. Though BTC open interest measured in BTC was actually down (-2.3%) since December 15, demand for speculation crept back into the markets as Bitcoin 90-day perp funding reached (4.8%), increasing from (3.7%) in mid-December.

Bitcoin Perp Funding has Trended Down Since October 2025

Onchain metrics were negative for Bitcoin, with most network health KPIs deteriorating, while other onchain metrics suggest that supply dynamics are improving. Some of the more concerning 30-day changes include daily network revenues (-15%), active addresses (-6%), new addresses (-4%), and active supply (+7%). These readings indicate that Bitcoin blockspace demand has fallen, with fewer new and existing users transferring value on the network. At the same time, the increase in active supply indicates that the breadth of Bitcoin holders churning their positions has increased.

Miners Pivot to AI as Hash Rate Declines

Bitcoin Hash Rate Suffers Longest Sustained Drop Since Spring 2024

Mining activity also continued to trend lower, with the 30-day average difficulty down (-2%) from 646 to 635, mirroring the drop in estimated global miner power consumption (-2%) from 206 TWh to 203 TWh. The 30-day moving-average hash rate is down (-6%) since its peak in mid-November 2025. The concurrent drop in difficulty, estimated power consumption, and hash rate suggests that miners are simply turning off their mining rigs. We partly attribute this dynamic to seasonal winter curtailment, with entities like Riot earning $6.2M in power credits, up (+113%) from November 2025 and (+520%) from December 2024.

However, we believe the larger, more systematic factor driving the reduction in hash rate stems from deteriorating economics for Bitcoin mining, as AI data center power demand explodes. We expect AI data center demand to persist over the coming years, with a (+24%) CAGR through 2030, and expect Bitcoin miners to increasingly devote power resources to servicing the buildout of artificial intelligence. As Ben Gagnon, CEO of Bitcoin miner Bitfarms, notes in a recent Wired interview, “It’s that HPC creates so much more value per unit of energy and does so predictably for years into the future that the company can’t justify further investment into bitcoin mining.”

Bitcoin holder dynamics offered a few bright spots that should provide investors with some comfort. Onchain transfer volume fell (-11%), while miner transfer volume to exchanges declined (-6%). Lower onchain transfer volume suggests reduced “churn” because less BTC is changing hands even as holder churn expands. We view the overall drop in BTC churn outweigh the breadth of changes cited above.

Bitcoin Long-Term Holder Resilience

BTC Supply Dormant >3 Yrs. Average Since 2020: ~38%

Over the last 30 days, we have observed positive net changes in Bitcoin positioning across most long-term holder cohorts. In our December ChainCheck, we note massive reductions in BTC dormancy amongst the 1-2yr, 2-3yr, and 3-5yr cohorts of (-900bps), (-1250bps), and (-550bps) m/m, respectively, whereas 5yr+ cohorts increased balances. Due to the medium-term sellers, the net effect was a total reduction in dormant supply of (-380bps) m/m in December.

By contrast, in January, we saw growth or deceleration in losses amongst the 1-2yr/2-3yr/3-5yr holder bands of (+205bps), (-174bps), and (-213bps) respectively. Additionally, BTC supply inactive >5 years ago was up (+176bps) over the past 30 days, with +95.5K BTC aging into these supply cohorts. From the trough on December 15, 2025, through January 14, 2026, the share of BTC not moved in over a year increased by (+69bps). Stepping back, middle-term holders are still hemorrhaging supply, but longer-term holders appear to be standing pat.

Bitcoin Digital Asset Treasury Mergers and Acquisitions

mNAVs De-Rated Prior to Bitcoin Price Declines

One of the most substantial challenges we believe Bitcoin faces is the potential liquidation of Bitcoin holdings by digital asset treasury companies (DATs). DATs use financial alchemy to buy more BTC in a strategy that gives shareholders recursive exposure to Bitcoin’s price movements. These companies’ primary KPI is to increase the amount of BTC held per share, which is achieved by issuing debt and equity securities to fund Bitcoin purchases. The best indicator of a DAT’s health is its mNAV (enterprise value/value of Bitcoin holdings), as this metric signals the DAT’s ability to continue purchasing Bitcoin through financing.

Trying to replicate the success of Strategy, many DATs formed in the spring and summer of 2025, and we estimate that the current number of “true” DATs holding more than 1k BTC to be 26. These DATs have gobbled up more than 867k BTC (4.3%) of the floating BTC supply, worth around $82.5B. However, since the mass entry of new BTC DATs, many DATs have seen their mNAVs falter. Of the 26 with more than 1k BTC, only 6 have mNAV > 1.0x. We believe this discount phenomenon is a substantial factor weighing on Bitcoin’s price due to the uncertainty around these entities persisting as going concerns. If these companies were forced to wind down, the result would be a surge in Bitcoin sales.

Thus, until these companies attain a healthy financial outlook, many investors fear a looming storm of Bitcoin being market-sold by the living estates of these companies. As these companies’ financial health pictures are recursive to the price of Bitcoin, if BTC goes up, it allows these companies to not only finance new Bitcoin purchases (pushing up BTC price), but also extend their operating runway. However, if Bitcoin prices continue their decline, the result could trigger reflexive selling of Bitcoin as DATs are dissolved in bankruptcy.

Options Open Interest ($M): MSTR vs. Mag7 and Indices

Asset Total Open Interest ($M) Put Open Interest ($M) Call Open Interest ($M)
MSTR 52,462 23,240 29,222
Magnificent 7
GOOG 183,133 83,956 99,178
META 177,105 65,658 111,448
NVDA 345,324 162,983 182,341
TSLA 328,103 147,499 180,604
AMZN 110,647 46,825 63,822
MSFT 117,045 46,679 70,367
AAPL 141,244 57,628 83,616
Indices
GLD 235,429 76,486 158,942
VNQ 433 288 145
IBIT 36,898 13,475 23,423
QQQ 580,155 351,856 228,299
BND 17 6 11
SPY 1,177,213 827,230 349,983

Source: Strategy as of 1/16/2026. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

However, there is another option for DATs, and that is consolidation. We have seen the beginning stages of the DAT merger activity, and we believe this trend will accelerate. Given the advantages offered to larger DATs, we expect only a few dominant DATs. This is because DATs must rely on liquidity in their debt, equity, and capital markets to absorb financing for Bitcoin purchases. The best liquidity is available to the biggest players. For example, although MSTR’s market cap is only 1/60th the average size of Mag7 stocks, its options open interest has at times exceeded that of Mag7 stocks. Therefore, MSTR has a tremendous financial advantage over competitors who cannot efficiently tap the financial markets. Over time, this dynamic should lead to persistent mNAV premiums for larger players compared to smaller ones.

As a result, we forecast that the best outcome for Bitcoin would be for the larger Bitcoin DATs to buy the smaller ones when those smaller entities trade at a discount. This dynamic would benefit both parties. While the shareholders of the acquirer would get access to cheaper Bitcoin than market-priced BTC to boost BTC per share, the owners of the acquiree would be able to recoup the discounted valuation of Bitcoin by getting a boosted mNAV. Of course, there is uncertainty over the definition of “fair tradeoff” for each party. However, the recently completed merger between Strive Inc. and Semler Scientific provides some insights.

Case Study: The Strive Inc. & Semler Scientific Merger

On September 22, 2025, around Bitcoin’s 2025 price peak, Strive Inc. (ASST) made Semler Scientific (SMLR) a lucrative merger offer: 21.05 ASST shares for 1 SMLR share. Initially, the deal was valued at $1.42B, representing a 210% premium to SMLR’s enterprise value. While the mNAV of SMLR was low (0.90), the premium implied by the deal was equivalent to buying BTC at ~190% of its then-market price. However, this excessive value was likely an overstatement, as ASST was trading at a very high mNAV of ~4x. Therefore, it was expected that the deal’s premium would decline as ASST’s mNAV declined to reflect the deal-related share dilution.

By New Year’s Eve 2025, Bitcoin had posted a few months of negative price action, falling (-20%) since the September deal date. Most of the DATs performed even worse. ASST shares fell (-73%) from the day before the merger announcement from $2.75 to $0.74, and mNAV dropped to 1.2x. SMLR shares were down (-49%), and mNAV deteriorated to 0.77x. As a result, the premium for the deal shrank to only (+1.6%) over market prices at that point in time.

Under those conditions, SMLR holders would be getting a modest mNAV lift to around 0.78x. Meanwhile, ASST holders would be improving their Bitcoin share exposure from 8.5 BTC per million shares to 10.4 BTC per million shares. However, on January 16, 2026, the deal closed with SMLR mNAV at 0.87. On the flip side, SMLR shareholders lost BTC per share exposure, moving from 332 BTC/m shares to 219 BTC/m shares. Meanwhile, ASST holders gave up mNAV, which moved from 1.37x to 1.06x the day after the merger. Thus, we can see the trade-offs each party was willing to make to get the deal done.

Potential Acquisition Targets: BTC Holdings and Valuation Metrics

Company BTC EV ($M) mNAV (EV) Domicile
Bitcoin Group SE 3,605 181 0.53 Germany
Empery Digital 4,081 273 0.7 US
Sequans Communications 2,264 111 0.52 France

Source: Bitcoin Treasuries Net as of 1/16/2026. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Looking ahead, we see great potential for the intrepid DAT to acquire another “Bitcoin company.” To us, three entities stand out: Bitcoin Group, Empery Digital, and Sequans Communications. Each of these companies has an mNAV below 1.0x while holding enough BTC to make the legal acquisition of each worthwhile. Of the bunch, we believe that the most likely acquiree is Empery Digital.

Bitcoin Group, based in Germany, has always been one of the most enigmatic crypto companies we cover. The stock trades at one of the steepest discounts in the peer group, even though the business appears profitable. The company reported earnings of €1.8 million in 2024. Bitcoin Group operates a crypto trading platform, Bitcoin.de, and a crypto custody business. The main hurdle is that the company owns and operates a regulated financial services entity in the EU, which makes it harder for someone to acquire it quickly or easily. An acquirer would need regulatory approval and a great deal of patience. Also, Bitcoin Group has an anchor shareholder, Priority AG, which holds>25% of the voting rights. However, given its juicy discount, it can be enticing to a sophisticated bidder who can navigate the pitfalls.

Empery Digital is nominally a “powersports/off-road vehicle” business. In practice, that operating unit is immaterial, generating only about $200k of earnings in 3Q2025. The more compelling feature is the sizable mNAV discount, but any prospective buyer has to contend with meaningful governance and anti-takeover friction.

Stockholders have limited ability to quickly change the board. Directors can be removed or replaced only at a duly scheduled meeting, and shareholders cannot call special meetings, sharply constraining rapid board turnover. That makes a proxy contest harder to run and harder to win on a tight timeline. On top of that, if an acquirer crosses 15%, Delaware law can restrict certain business combination activity for three years, effectively delaying a merger path. The board also has the ability to issue preferred shares, which could be used to dilute or otherwise deter an unsolicited bid.

That said, a patient DAT-style acquirer could simply wait for the next annual meeting in May 2026. If the mNAV discount persists, the run-up to that meeting could get interesting. Overall, Empery looks like a better candidate than Bitcoin Group given the more familiar jurisdiction, but any M&A path is more likely to be a process than an imminent event.

The first issue with Sequans is structural complexity. The company is based in France, but it owns subsidiaries across multiple jurisdictions, including the UK, US, Singapore, Israel, and Finland. Any buyer is stepping into a multi-country setup with more moving parts and more opportunities for process risk to surface.

Sequans also has a real operating business. It is a fabless semiconductor company that was profitable in 2024, reporting about $57 million in net income. That matters because an acquirer cannot treat this as a simple balance-sheet buy. The operating business would need to be valued on its own and either managed long-term or separated, which likely pushes you toward a more complex structure like a carve-out or spin or forces the buyer to run a business they may not know well.

On the takeover side, France adds another hurdle. Under French takeover rules, crossing roughly 30% ownership can trigger a mandatory tender offer. That makes it difficult to build a stake quietly and then move fast, and it can turn the process into something slower and more procedural. The board also has tools that can make life harder for an outside buyer, including the ability to issue shares in ways that dilute a potential acquirer or favor friendly parties. Similar to Empery, board and shareholder actions run on set meeting timelines, which adds time and market risk.

Finally, because this is a semiconductor business, it can draw government scrutiny. French authorities can review and potentially restrict foreign buyers, which adds another layer of uncertainty on top of everything else. Given the many challenges confronting potential buyers, Sequans is the least attractive of the small group.

data.title: Alger Mid Cap Focus Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alger Mid Cap Focus Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Class A shares of the Alger Mid Cap Focus Fund (ALOAX) underperformed the Russell Midcap Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Exact Sciences Corporation (EXAS), Natera, Inc. (NTRA), and Guardant Health, Inc. (GH) were among the top contributors to performance.

  • Exact Sciences is a molecular diagnostics company focused on oncology testing, best known for its Cologuard noninvasive colorectal cancer screening franchise, alongside a broader portfolio and pipeline that includes molecular residual disease (MRD) and multi-cancer early detection (MCED) testing initiatives. Shares contributed positively during the quarter after Abbott announced a definitive all-cash agreement to acquire Exact Sciences for approximately $21 billion in equity value, reflecting a meaningful premium and driving the stock higher.
  • Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.
  • Guardant Health is a molecular diagnostics company specializing in liquid biopsy tests for oncology applications. Unlike traditional tissue-based tests, Guardant’s liquid biopsies use blood samples, enabling faster results and improved patient comfort. The company’s tests include comprehensive genomic profiling, cancer diagnosis, and treatment monitoring. Shares contributed to performance after the company delivered strong fiscal third- quarter operating results and raised full-year 2025 revenue guidance, signaling stronger-than-expected momentum across Oncology, Screening, and Biopharma & Data.

Roblox Corp. (RBLX), Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD), and Nebius Group (NBIS) were among the top detractors from performance.

  • Roblox is a digital platform where users play, learn, communicate, and expand relationships in millions of 3D digital experiences within an open-world metaverse environment. The platform is powered by user-generated content (UGC) with experiences and most items being built by third-party developers and creators, on top of Roblox’s development engine. Roblox uses AI to improve recommendations, moderate user-generated content, and help creators build new experiences more efficiently. The company reports bookings when users purchase its virtual currency, Robux, through one-time purchases, prepaid cards, or subscriptions. Shares detracted during the quarter following management’s decision to use the surge in engagement and monetization to reinvest into the business through higher capital expenditures and operating expenses. The company plans to allocate capital into building and training their own AI models along with incorporating additional safeguards to weed out inappropriate activities.
  • Robinhood is a consumer-focused financial platform offering commission-free trading in stocks, options, futures and crypto, alongside retirement investing and subscription benefits via Robinhood Gold. The company has recently broadened into wealth management with Robinhood Strategies (expert-managed portfolios), and announced Robinhood Banking and Robinhood Cortex as part of a wider push to deliver advisory, banking and AI-driven tools within the app. The company’s revenue mix comes from payment for order flow and commissions, cryptocurrency, equities revenues, futures, net interest income from margin lending and interest earned on customer cash balances, securities lending, and credit cards. During the quarter, shares detracted from performance, primarily due to a sharp decline in cryptocurrency markets.
  • Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

The views expressed are the views of Fred Alger Management, LLC (“FAM”) and its affiliates as of December 2025. Growth stocks may be more volatile than other stocks as their prices tend to be higher in relation to their companies’ earnings and may be more sensitive to market, political, and economic developments. data.title: The Magnum Ice Cream Company: Market Dominance With The Strongest Brands
data.description:
Summary

I give a buy rating to The Magnum Ice Cream Company (MICC) because it has the strongest brand portfolio in global ice cream, has significant scale, and benefits from structural advantages in both retail and away-from-home channels.

Company overview

MICC is a company that most readers would know. It is headquartered in Amsterdam and has a few key brands: Magnum, Ben & Jerry’s, Cornetto, and Wall’s. It operates in more than 80 countries with 30 manufacturing facilities and is the only true pure-play ice cream company listed on the public markets.

Portfolio brands and market share

Fundamentally, I think the most attractive aspect of MICC is its market share and brands. It owns five of the top ten global ice cream brands, which gives it a strong advantage in pricing power. Taste is important, but I think it is the brand equity that really helps MICC to push cost increases to consumers, especially given that input costs for cocoa and dairy can be volatile. The key here is consumer mindshare. Consumers that love eating Ben & Jerry's will go back to it even if prices go up because there is no direct substitute for that specific product experience. Of course, there is a limit to how much MICC can push through prices, as with all consumer-facing products, but relative to unknown brands, MICC is in a much better position.

The worth of MICC’s strong brand equity can also be seen from its market share. It is the largest player in the industry and leads by a mile. At 21% market share, it is close to 2x the next pure-play ice cream player (Froneri) and is larger than the rest of the next 7 players combined. This scale gives a structural cost advantage as it moves more volume than anyone else in the industry. Any related fixed cost would be amortized across a larger customer base, and variable costs can be driven down given the sheer scale of procurement size. You can argue that some of these other competitors are also quite large (in terms of overall company revenue and not just ice cream), which gives them similar bargaining power. I think that is true, but remember that MICC is not just competing against these players; there are a ton of fragmented sub-scale players out there that simply cannot replicate this cost advantage.

This market dominance also gives MICC an advantage when it comes to dealing with retailers. If you are a major supermarket chain like Walmart, you are “forced” to sell the large brands like Magnum or Cornetto in the ice cream section to lure in foot traffic. And since MICC is the only supplier for this, it basically makes MICC a "must-have" supplier, and that dynamic gives MICC a lot of leverage to secure prime shelf placement and defend against new players. The brutal reality in the retail world is that whoever has the most shelf space basically wins half the battle. A new player placing its ice cream products at the far end of the store corner will not get the same attention as one that is right at the door.

Away-from-Home Growth

Outside of retail shelf space, MICC is also dominant in the Away-from-Home channel, and more specifically, the freezer cabinet network. I have no doubts that MICC will continue to stay dominant here as it expands its freezer cabinet installed base. This freezer cabinet strategy is just not about placing a fridge with MICC’s product inside; there is a strong strategic value in doing so. A branded freezer is basically a controlled shelf space. When MICC installs a Wall’s cabinet in a convenience store in, say, Brazil, that point of sale (that freezer and that particular spot) is effectively locked in to sell only MICC products. This is especially great at targeting impulse ice cream buying since availability matters more than brand preference. Consumers buy what is in front of them when the impulse moment comes. Hence, by increasing cabinet density, MICC is effectively locking in more volume.

The other strategic implication of having a larger installed base is also the marketing value of "eyeball share." Each of these cabinets effectively functions as a permanent billboard. Whether a consumer buys an ice cream or not, they are constantly exposed to MICC’s brand logo, which I believe helps keep consumer mindshare high without requiring additional marketing spend.

Valuation

Right now, MICC trades at about 14.5x NTM PE, a discount to larger peers like Nestle, JDE Peet’s, and Danone (18x, 17x, and 16.7x, respectively). This discount is surprising because all of them have the same revenue growth outlook of mid-single digits. Net margin-wise, MICC is also not far behind the latter two (Nestle is a much bigger giant with a higher net margin). MICC also deserves some form of premium, given it is the dominant player in the ice cream industry.

I believe the discount has more to do with the technical aspect of things, specifically, the overhang of the 19.9% stake held by Unilever. Putting aside my bullish view on the fundamentals, I agree this could create a temporary ceiling as investors are worried their share price may see pressure if Unilever offloads. But I don’t think it is that big of an issue since it is also in Unilever’s interest that MICC grow and do well so they can maximize their eventual exit value. In that sense, so long as MICC can grow the business, these overhangs should not be an issue over the longer term.

Let’s also not forget this is a very cash-generative business, and management has been clear they will pay out 40 to 60% of net profit as dividends over the medium term. Based on current consensus estimates, MICC is set to pay out EUR 50 cents of DPS ($0.59) in FY26, which is about a 3.5% yield. As the market realizes MICC deserves to trade in line with peers over time (when MICC grows and continues to maintain market share leadership), we could be looking at ~17% upside if multiples re-rate to 17x, plus all the dividends collected while waiting.

Investment Risk

Volatile input costs, specifically cocoa and dairy, are always a risk. While hedging can help to mitigate near-term impact, prolonged inflation would eventually pressure gross margins. The ice cream industry has a low barrier to entry, so there would be continuous competitive pressure. If MICC does not keep up with innovation or maintain sufficient shelf space advantage, resulting in brand equity weakness, it could see much lower growth over time.

Conclusion

MICC is long, in my view. This is a rare pure-play ice cream player with strong brands, cost advantages, and a dominant market position. The freezer cabinet network helps to capture volume and also market the brand, while brand dominance helps with pricing power and getting more shelf space.

data.title: Top 10 Dividend Stocks For Uncertain Times
data.description:
When Market Volatility Strikes

A global sell-off erased early gains the S&P 500 posted in 2026, driven by U.S. trade tensions with Europe along with geopolitical friction over Greenland. Stress in Japan’s bond market also contributed to the renewed volatility, which comes on top of sticky inflation, mixed labor signals, and concerns over elevated valuations.

In periods like this, a fixed-income source can help relieve investor anxiety by offering a steady stream of cash and downside protection. Dividends and reinvestment have contributed significantly to shareholder returns throughout the ups and downs of economic cycles.

Protecting your portfolio requires more than buying high-yield stocks - safety, growth, and consistency are critical factors in finding the best opportunities. Seeking Alpha’s Dividend Grades have demonstrated accuracy in avoiding cuts and delivering growth. The grading system flags dividends at risk by analyzing crucial indicators such as payout ratio, interest coverage, cash flow, operating leverage, and debt levels.

SA Quant assembled a list of 10 Strong Buy dividend stocks that are well-equipped for potentially navigating market uncertainty. The list showcases collective strength across dividend yield and excellent all-around investment fundamentals like momentum, profitability, and earnings revisions.

Using Seeking Alpha’s Stock Screener, I filtered for stocks built for uncertain market conditions, including strengths across dividend safety, growth, yield, and consistency. This basket of 10 stocks has an average dividend yield of ~4%. By comparison, the S&P 500 (SPY) yields 1.05%, and the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) 1.58%. Alpine Income Property Trust is a high-yielding REIT with a solid track record of growth, offering a diverse portfolio of 128 properties across 34 states, boasting a 99% occupancy rate. The portfolio features top tenants such as Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW), DICK'S Sporting Goods, Inc. (DKS), and Walgreens.

Rent escalations and higher interest income boosted revenue and earnings growth as Alpine stepped up the size and pace of investments. Alpine continued to enhance the credit quality of its portfolio, and by the end of Q3 2025, Lowe's and Dick's Sporting Goods represented 48% of annualized base rent.

Robust AFFO generation over the past five years supports Alpine’s consistent dividend, which offers an attractive yield vs. the real estate sector median. PINE’s dividend has a 5Y growth rate of 6.81% and has been paid out consistently since its 2019 IPO.

data.title: Why Tight Stop-Losses Often Hurt Investors - And What Robust Capital Growth Really Requires
data.description:
By Xinyu Xiong

Ask investors how they manage risk, and many will give the same answer: tight stop-losses. Widely viewed as a cornerstone of disciplined risk management, tight stop-losses can sometimes work against investors’ long-term objectives.

A stop-loss is a predefined rule that forces the exit of an investment position when its price moves against the investor by a specified amount. Its primary purpose is to limit downside losses on an individual position without requiring continuous monitoring. The rationale seems straightforward. By limiting losses on individual positions, investors believe they are exercising discipline and protecting the portfolio from severe drawdowns.

More broadly, the issue touches on three related questions in risk management: the trade-off between precision and robustness, how trade-level rules aggregate into portfolio-level outcomes, and why controls designed for psychological comfort can impair long-term compounding.

In practice, many who rigorously apply tight stop-loss rules experience a frustrating pattern: frequent small losses, occasional gains, and little progress toward durable capital growth. This raises a critical question for long-term investors, portfolio managers, and fiduciaries alike: can widely accepted stop-loss practices be structurally counterproductive? And what can they be replaced with?

When Trade-Level Discipline Conflicts with Portfolio Outcomes

Viewed in isolation, tight stop-losses appear prudent. By defining a small, predetermined loss, investors feel they have transformed uncertainty into something measurable and controllable. Each trade appears safe in isolation, and losses feel disciplined rather than accidental. This provides investors with a level of psychological comfort.

Markets, however, do not reward isolated decisions. They reward sequences of decisions made under uncertainty. In trend-based or breakout strategies (e.g., when an asset or stock moves beyond its target price), profitable opportunities rarely develop smoothly. Early phases are often volatile, marked by reversals and false starts. Narrow stop-losses systematically remove investors during precisely this stage, not because the underlying signal is invalid, but because short-term price fluctuations exceed arbitrarily tight thresholds.

Once stopped out, re-entry is difficult. Recent losses discourage recommitment to the same trade, and prices may have already moved away from the original entry point. The result is a portfolio that avoids large losses but also misses the handful of outsized gains that drive long-term returns.

What looks like good risk control at the trade level can become opportunity destruction at the portfolio level.

The Behavioral Appeal and Cost of Tight Stops

The case against tight stop-losses has become stronger as markets themselves have changed. Modern markets are dominated by algorithmic trading, fragmented liquidity, and automated execution. Prices now move faster, liquidity is more conditional, and short-term volatility is often driven by order flow dynamics rather than information. In this environment, stop-losses behave differently than they did in slower, dealer-driven markets.

The popularity of tight stop-losses reflects their psychological appeal. By defining a small, predetermined loss, investors feel a sense of control. Losses appear disciplined rather than accidental, and regret is minimized, at least in the short term.

But this comfort comes at a cost. Tight stop-losses align closely with behavioral biases such as loss aversion and regret avoidance. They optimize for emotional relief rather than economic outcomes. Markets, however, reward sustained exposure to favorable return distributions, not psychological comfort.

Risk Management is Also About Time in the Market

Discussions about stop-losses often focus narrowly on loss size. But risk is not only about how much is lost when an investment fails, it is also about how long capital remains exposed to opportunity.

Exposure persistence matters because capital growth is multiplicative. Long-term performance depends not only on avoiding losses but on remaining invested long enough to participate in sustained price movements. Truncating exposure too aggressively can be just as damaging as taking excessive losses.

To examine this trade-off more clearly, it helps to move beyond individual trades and decompose performance into three components:

  • Position size
  • Win rate
  • Payoff ratio (average gain relative to average loss)

Stop-loss design directly affects both win rate and payoff ratio — often in opposing directions.

What the Evidence Suggests

To make these trade-offs concrete, it is useful to examine how stop-loss width affects portfolio outcomes when other variables are held constant. Specifically, consider a simple long-only trend-entry framework applied to a broad equity index. Positions are initiated when prices cross above a moving average. Position size is held constant, while stop-loss thresholds vary from very tight to relatively wide levels.

Using daily S&P 500 (SPX) open, high, low, and close prices as a data source, I simulate 500 investors entering at random dates (2000–2005) and compare outcomes under different stop-loss widths and take-profit targets (15%–30%). Each curve summarizes the average result across investors (Figure 1).

The objective is not to identify an optimal trading rule or maximize historical returns. Instead, the goal is to examine how stop-loss width structurally influences win rates, payoff ratios, and cumulative capital growth.

As stop-losses widen, win rates increase. Trades are given more room to absorb short-term noise, reducing premature exits.

Figure 1: Win Rate as a Function of Stop-Loss Width

At the same time, when stop-losses are set farther away from the entry price, the average size of losses increases relative to the average size of gains.

Figure 2: Payoff Ratio as a Function of Stop-Loss Width

When these effects are combined at the portfolio level, cumulative returns plotted against stop-loss width reveal a striking asymmetry: a single peak surrounded by a broad, uneven plateau. Performance deteriorates sharply when stop-losses are too tight but declines only gradually when they are moderately widened beyond the optimal point. This asymmetry is especially evident when higher take-profit targets are considered.

Figure 3: Cumulative Return as a Function of Stop-Loss Width

Why Robustness Matters More Than Precision

The existence of an optimal stop-loss level does not mean it must be identified with precision. Performance is highly fragile on the left side of the return curve, where stop-losses are too tight and small estimation errors, execution frictions, or regime shifts can have outsized negative effects.

On the right side, cumulative returns form a broad plateau. Moderate increases in stop-loss width do not materially impair long-term performance.

This asymmetry suggests a shift in perspective. Robust capital growth is achieved not by operating at the point of maximum expected return but by remaining within a range of parameter resilience.

Accepting slightly wider stop-losses may increase single-trade drawdowns, but it also reduces sensitivity to noise, uncertainty, and behavioral frictions, unavoidable features of real-world investing.

Implications for Long-Term Investors

Tight stop-losses are often perceived as disciplined risk control, but they can unintentionally undermine long-term performance by truncating exposure and amplifying behavioral frictions. In modern markets, robust risk management focuses less on where the stop is placed and more on how exits are structured, timed, and executed.

Rather than asking how tight a stop-loss can be made, investors may benefit from reframing the question:

  • Does this stop-loss allow sufficient time for an opportunity to develop?
  • Am I optimizing for precision or for robustness?
  • Am I minimizing losses or maximizing participation in favorable return distributions?
  • Can I tolerate larger individual losses in exchange for more stable long-term growth?

The Upshot

Risk management is not about eliminating discomfort. It is about choosing which discomforts are worth enduring. By recognizing the structural trade-off between win rate and payoff ratio, and by prioritizing robustness over narrow optimization, investors can design stop-loss frameworks that better align with the realities of market behavior and the mathematics of capital growth.
